core_intuitions: Why a loss spike amplifies: on the spike step Adam's second moment sqrt(v_hat) is still STALE (beta2 near 1, updates slowly), so the update scales with the raw gradient ratio (~100x normal); over the next few hundred steps sqrt(v_hat) absorbs the spike, the denominator grows, updates damp, and the spike is usually transient.

The spike causal chain: bad batch OR bad state -> large gradient -> large parameter update -> bad model state (wrong basin) -> elevated loss; every mitigation is just a valve that severs one specific link of this chain.
Gradient clipping caps amplification by roughly one order of magnitude (100x -> 10x) by rescaling on the GLOBAL norm while preserving gradient direction.

Attention-logit overflow: an unbounded QK^T/sqrt(d_k) drives a peaked softmax whose exp-sum underflows into NaN gradients; QK-Norm (and z-loss) bound the logits at the source so the softmax stays well-conditioned.
Diagnosis is signal-driven: the value of grad_norm (normal / large / NaN / near-zero) together with LR timing discriminates a data cause from an optimizer, floating-point, init, or warmup cause.

Smooth loss is not proof of health: data corruption (dedup bugs, repetition) can degrade the model with no visible spike, so grad_norm, activation/logit maxima, and per-domain eval must be watched separately.

new_figures.name: adam-spike-amplification
new_figures.concept: The temporal mechanism of a loss spike: Adam's fast first moment reacts while its slow second-moment denominator lags, producing a ~100x update that then fades as the denominator catches up.
new_figures.rationale: This is the deepest and most-referenced intuition in the chapter (the causal chain, the worked example, 'spikes are usually transient', the partial-recovery story all rest on it). The essential idea is a LAG in time between numerator and denominator and a recovery over subsequent steps, which a single static frame cannot convey.
new_figures.brief: A timeline of discrete optimizer steps along the x-axis, three horizontal tracks stacked. TOP track: incoming per-parameter gradient g as vertical bars, small and roughly constant (~0.01, historical RMS) for a run of normal steps, then ONE tall bar (~1.0, labeled 'bad batch = 100x historical RMS') in --bad, then returning to small. MIDDLE: show the update rule once, |dtheta| approx alpha*(1-beta1)*g / sqrt(v_hat), with the numerator term annotated 'reacts THIS step (1-beta1 = 0.1)' and the denominator sqrt(v_hat) annotated 'slow / stale (beta2 = 0.999, 1-beta2 approx 1e-3)'; draw sqrt(v_hat) as a line that stays flat and small (~0.01) THROUGH the spike step and only rises gradually over the following steps ('catches up'). BOTTOM track: resulting update magnitude |dtheta| as bars: normal steps approx 3e-5 at baseline height labeled '1x'; the spike step approx 3e-3 as a dramatically tall bar labeled '100x - because sqrt(v_hat) has not absorbed the spike yet'; subsequent bars shrinking back toward baseline as sqrt(v_hat) rises. On the spike step also draw a dashed ghost bar in --good/--accent labeled 'with grad clip tau=1.0: gradient rescaled ~0.1x -> update approx 3e-4 = 10x' so clipping's order-of-magnitude reduction is visible. A small inset loss curve to the side ticks UP at the spike then partially recovers, labeled 'transient'. Animation stages: (1) normal steps tick by, denominator line flat; (2) spike gradient arrives, denominator still stale, bottom bar shoots up to 100x; (3) reveal the clipped ghost bar capping at 10x; (4) sqrt(v_hat) line climbs over the next steps, update bars shrink, loss inset recovers. MUST be fully legible when frozen on stage 2 (the spike moment). Theme-safe: --bad for the spike, --good or --accent for the clipped/recovery path, --muted for guides/axes, currentColor for text. Use ONLY the chapter's own illustrative integers (0.01, 1.0, 0.1, 0.999, 3e-5, 3e-3, 3e-4, 100x, 10x); invent no benchmark numbers.

new_figures.name: spike-causal-chain-mitigations
new_figures.concept: The five-link spike causal chain with each mitigation shown as a valve that severs one specific link.
new_figures.rationale: Organizes the entire mitigations half of the chapter (architectural, training-time, data) into one map: the reader sees that clipping, skip-batch, QK-Norm, z-loss, init, and embedding-clip are not a grab-bag but each interrupt a different stage of the same chain. Turns a list of techniques into a single causal picture. A static frame suffices.
new_figures.brief: A left-to-right horizontal chain of five boxes joined by bold arrows: 'bad batch OR bad state' -> 'large gradient' -> 'large parameter update' -> 'bad model state (wrong basin)' -> 'elevated loss'. Above the gradient->update transition, a callout annotation: 'Adam amplifies here: |dtheta| proportional to g / sqrt(v_hat), stale denominator' (ties to the amplification figure). Attach labelled INTERCEPTION badges (a small valve or scissors/cut mark) to the exact link each technique severs: at the SOURCE (first box / its inputs) place 'skip anomalous batch (data audit)', 'QK-Norm - bounds attention logits', 'z-loss - bounds output logits', 'careful init: depth-scale 1/sqrt(2L), small embedding', 'embedding-norm clip'; on the gradient->update ARROW place 'gradient clipping tau=1.0 (rescale global norm, keep direction)'; on the update BOX place 'skip-step if grad_norm > 5x tau'. Group the badges with a small legend of three categories - Architectural (act at source), Training-time (act on gradient/update), Data (act on the batch) - each category given a distinct theme-safe surface tint / border color (--accent, --good, a neutral surface), NOT hardcoded hues. Reading order strictly left (cause) to right (consequence), with mitigations reading as valves cutting the flow. Keep every label legible and inside the viewBox; avoid overlapping the badges by stacking them vertically under their link. Theme-safe vars only; use the chapter's own values (tau=1.0, 5x, 1/sqrt(2L)), no invented numbers.

new_figures.name: attention-logit-overflow-qknorm
new_figures.concept: How an unbounded QK logit drives a peaked softmax that underflows to NaN, and how QK-Norm bounds the logit so the softmax stays stable.
new_figures.rationale: QK-Norm is the chapter's #1 recommended architectural mitigation and the subject of a full war story (fp16 head-7 NaN cascade). The failure is a specific multi-stage pathway (large norms -> huge logit -> collapsed softmax -> underflow -> NaN) that is far clearer as a contrasted pair of pipelines than as prose.
new_figures.brief: Two parallel horizontal pathways sharing the same start. TOP pathway 'Without QK-Norm' (ends in --bad): stage 1 - Q and K vectors both with large L2 norm (~100) drawn as long vectors; stage 2 - dot product scaled by 1/sqrt(d_k) with d_k=128, annotate 1/sqrt(128) approx 0.088; stage 3 - resulting logit approx 100*100*0.088 approx 880, annotate 'within fp16 max 65504 - but far too peaked'; stage 4 - a softmax bar chart that COLLAPSES to a single tall spike on one token with all others approx 0; stage 5 - endpoint marked --bad: 'exp-sum underflows -> NaN gradient cascade'. Draw the fp16 ceiling 65504 as a faint --muted reference line and make explicit that the real danger is the peaked softmax + underflow, not raw overflow of 880. BOTTOM pathway 'With QK-Norm' (ends in --good): same Q,K -> RMSNorm(Q), RMSNorm(K) shown shrinking/normalizing the vectors so their norm is bounded -> logits now O(sqrt(d_k)), a modest range -> softmax drawn as a smooth, spread distribution -> endpoint marked --good: 'well-conditioned softmax, stable gradients'. A short caption anchors it to the chapter's Story 3 (fp16, head 7, logits near 60000). Theme-safe: --bad for the failure endpoint and collapsed bar, --good for the stable path, --muted for the reference line, currentColor for text/vectors. Use ONLY the chapter's illustrative integers (128, 0.088, 100, 880, 65504); no invented benchmark numbers. Everything inside the viewBox, the two pathways clearly separated top/bottom.

new_figures.name: spike-diagnostic-decision-tree
new_figures.concept: Reading grad_norm (plus LR timing) to narrow a loss spike to its root-cause category.
new_figures.rationale: The operational heart of the debugging half of the chapter: a single number, grad_norm, discriminates data vs optimizer vs floating-point vs init causes. The chapter currently conveys this only as ASCII text; a scannable tree makes the 3-AM decision fast and is the payoff of the whole playbook section.
new_figures.brief: A clean top-down decision tree rooted at 'Loss spike detected'. The FIRST and visually-emphasized fork is on the key signal grad_norm (emphasize it as the primary discriminator with a bold node), with four labelled outcomes, each a rounded leaf card: (a) 'grad_norm normal (<2x clip) + loss spiked' -> category DATA: 'anomalous batch (repetition / encoding / dedup bug)' -> fix: 'skip-batch, audit the source shard'; (b) 'grad_norm large (>5x clip)' -> category OPTIMIZER: 'Adam amplification, stale sqrt(v_hat)' -> fix: 'clip, reduce LR ~20-30%, resume from pre-spike ckpt'; (c) 'grad_norm NaN / Inf' -> category FLOATING-POINT: 'activation/attention overflow' -> fix: 'QK-Norm, switch to bf16, add z-loss'; (d) 'grad_norm approx 0' -> category VANISHING / BAD INIT -> fix: 'check depth-scaling & init'. Add a separate cross-cutting condition node off to the side: 'Spike coincides with LR peak / end of warmup?' -> leaf 'WARMUP too short: extend to >=1% of steps, use sqrt-warmup'. Color-code the four category leaves with theme-safe surface tints and accent/good/bad borders (DATA neutral, OPTIMIZER --accent, FLOATING-POINT --bad, VANISHING --muted). Keep the tree shallow and scannable; the root grad_norm node should read as 'this one number tells you which branch'. Theme-safe vars only; use the chapter's own multiples (2x, 5x clip, >=1%), invent no numbers. All cards legible and inside the viewBox.

summary: Chapter 3.11 is concept-rich but had zero figures. Its intuitions cluster into a mechanism half (why spikes amplify via Adam's stale second moment, the causal chain, QK-logit overflow) and an operations half (signal-driven diagnosis, monitoring). I propose four foundational figures and no audits (none exist). Two priority-1: a long-animation of the Adam denominator lag (the deepest intuition, genuinely temporal - numerator reacts, sqrt(v_hat) lags then catches up, clipping caps 100x->10x), and a static causal-chain map showing each mitigation as a valve severing one link (organizes the entire mitigations half). Two priority-2 statics: the attention-logit-overflow vs QK-Norm contrasted pathway (the #1 recommended mitigation + Story 3), and a grad_norm-keyed diagnostic decision tree (upgrades the ASCII playbook into the scannable operational core). All briefs are theme-safe and reuse only the chapter's own illustrative integers.
